political_offices.title: Regent
political_offices.jurisdiction: University of the State of New York

notable.notable_blurb: U.S. Congressman, New York circuit judge, Litchfield Law

narrative_blocks.header: U.S. Congressman, circuit judge, and rector of Troy
narrative_blocks.detail: Cushman prepared for Yale at Plainfield Academy alongside John Adams (Yale 1795) and read law under Judge Zephaniah Swift of Windham before arriving at Litchfield. At Litchfield he became close friends with William L. Marcy — who went on to serve as U.S. Senator, Governor of New York, Secretary of War, and Secretary of State. Cushman's Litchfield Law School classmate John Allen Collier gave a memorial address at the opening of the Supreme Court after his death. He was also a trustee of Union College from 1833 until his death, and a regent of New York University. His father-in-law was Colonel Benjamin Tallmadge, Washington's spymaster. (Wikipedia; Litchfield Historical Society)
narrative_blocks.body: John Paine Cushman graduated Yale in 1807, studied at the Litchfield Law School in 1808, and was admitted to the bar in 1809. He was elected to the 15th Congress (1817-1819) as a Federalist, then served as Recorder of Troy (1834-1838) and Judge of the Third Circuit Court (1838-1844). Upon his death in 1848, the U.S. Supreme Court adjourned in his honor.

narrative_blocks.detail: The mansion remained in the family's orbit through much of the 19th century. Maria's father Colonel Benjamin Tallmadge was a frequent correspondent; their social circle included the leading legal and political figures of early New York State. (Landmark; YouTube documentary)
narrative_blocks.body: The Cushman family home at 39 First Street in Troy, built in 1820, is a four-story all-brick Federal-style house. Its original owners were direct descendants of Revolutionary War heroes and Plymouth Colony settlers.

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended 1808; admitted bar 1809; permanently settled Troy NY.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His 1820 Troy townhouse, near the Washington Park he helped found.

(Wikipedia; Litchfield Historical Society)

Upon his death in 1848, the U.S. Supreme Court adjourned in his honor.

notable.notable_blurb: Litchfield Female Academy; daughter of Culper Ring's Benjamin

narrative_blocks.header: Student at the Litchfield Female Academy
narrative_blocks.detail: Maxcy's friend Lemuel Williams wrote to him on Christmas Eve 1808, the week before his own wedding: 'O! my Virgil! How ardently I wish that we might all meet together, you & your Maria, I and my Sarah, in the bonds of wedded love.' The letter suggests Maria had already ended the engagement by then. Cushman and Maxcy had been classmates at Litchfield Law School — making the social circle of early American legal education small enough that broken hearts and new marriages overlapped by a season. (Galloway-Maxcy-Markoe Papers, Library of Congress; Litchfield Historical Society)
narrative_blocks.body: Maria Jones Tallmadge studied at the Litchfield Female Academy in 1801 and 1802. Before meeting John Cushman, she was engaged to Virgil Maxcy, a Litchfield Law School student. A surviving letter from Tapping Reeve consoling Maria suggests the break came around 1807 — after which John Cushman, who had just arrived at the same law school, became her husband in 1812.

narrative_blocks.detail: Benjamin Tallmadge is credited with uncovering the treason of Benedict Arnold and thwarting the British capture of West Point. He corresponded with his son-in-law John Cushman on legal matters throughout the 1820s. A 1824 letter survives in which Tallmadge wrote to Cushman about a domestic incident at his Litchfield home — a reminder that even the Culper Ring's spymaster kept up a lively family correspondence. (Litchfield Historical Society; Wikipedia on Benjamin Tallmadge)
narrative_blocks.body: Maria's father was Colonel Benjamin Tallmadge, Yale 1773, who ran General Washington's spy network — the Culper Ring — during the Revolutionary War. He later served as a U.S. Congressman. Her maternal grandfather was General William Floyd, member of the Continental Congress and signer of the Declaration of Independence.

cross_connections.display_label: is the mother of Edwards Williams, who married Mary Floyd Cushman

narrative_blocks.body: Maria died in 1824, aged about four.

death.cause: Died of apoplexy. Body taken to Troy NY for burial.

notable.notable_blurb: Civil War ordnance officer; wrote an early flag pledge

narrative_blocks.detail: Balch served in the Ordnance Bureau (September 1863–September 1864), where his administrative skill kept the department functioning through intense political infighting between Stanton and Ramsey. He then taught at West Point (1864–65) and commanded the Charleston Arsenal after the war, helping restore the facility to functionality. He later worked for the Erie Railway, conducting an audit so thorough he discovered forgotten properties worth approximately one million dollars. In 1886 Mayor Hewitt of New York City appointed him to investigate fraud in the Health Department and street paving contracts. (Biographical notes)
narrative_blocks.body: George Thacher Balch graduated West Point in 1852, served at Fort Pickens, Florida in April 1861, and was drawn into one of the Civil War's most unusual command disputes. Secretary of War Edwin Stanton wanted Balch as Chief of Ordnance; President Lincoln wanted Colonel Ramsey. The compromise gave Ramsey the title and Balch the actual authority.

narrative_blocks.detail: Balch encountered large numbers of foreign-born students and saw a need for shared civic rituals that could foster American identity. He spent the last five years of his life promoting his pledge in New York City schools. When Bellamy published his version in 1892, the debate over which pledge to use continued for years. Balch died in 1894, before the Bellamy version was nationally adopted. (Biographical notes)
narrative_blocks.body: In his role with the New York City Board of Education, Balch developed a patriotic pledge and flag-salute ritual for schoolchildren — specifically to reach immigrant children — that predated Francis Bellamy's 1892 Pledge of Allegiance. The pledge read: 'I give my heart and my hand to my country — one country, one language, one flag.'

military_service.notes: Effectively directed the Ordnance Bureau 1863-64 with Secretary Stanton's backing; later commanded the Charleston Arsenal.

notable.notable_blurb: Founder of the Mendocino Lumber Company

narrative_blocks.header: He founded the Mendocino Lumber Company
narrative_blocks.body: Williams surveyed Big River for its redwoods in 1852 and crossed Panama to buy milling machinery shipped around Cape Horn. He founded and long led the Mendocino Lumber Company, the redwood mill that built the coastal town.

narrative_blocks.header: A New York Volunteer in the Mexican War
narrative_blocks.body: Williams served with the 1st New York Volunteers in the Mexican-American War and reached California in 1847, on the eve of the Gold Rush. He lived to witness the 1906 San Francisco earthquake from his lumber company's ruined offices.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a distant Hooker cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Williams and his wife Mary Floyd Cushman both descended from the Reverend Thomas Hooker through separate colonial lines, he through the Edwards family and she through the Tallmadges. Their daughters thus carried a double descent from Hartford's founder.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: The Big River redwood mill he founded, now in Mendocino Headlands State Park.

bio.bio_blurb: Founder of the Mendocino Lumber Company

narrative_blocks.body: Sarah died in 1863, aged about one.

notable.notable_blurb: First woman history PhD at UC Berkeley

narrative_blocks.header: First woman to earn a Berkeley history doctorate
narrative_blocks.body: At fifty-two Williams became the first woman to complete a history doctorate at the University of California, with a landmark study of San Francisco's 1851 Vigilance Committee. The dissertation was published by the University of California Press in 1921.

narrative_blocks.header: Librarian and historian of Gold Rush California
narrative_blocks.body: Williams helped found California library science and was among the first readers admitted to the new Huntington Library. She lectured on California history and traveled widely across Asia, illustrating her talks with her own photographs.

cross_connections.display_label: her cousin and namesake, the missionary doctor in Angola
among the first American women to earn a doctorate
also led the California Historical Society as president
a Hooker descendant who studied in New York, then Miami

bio.bio_blurb: First woman history PhD at UC Berkeley

institution_affiliations.notes: Served in Chilesso, Angola for 20 years. Established hospital staffed by indigenous Angolan people she trained.

notable.notable_blurb: First woman on Maine State Medical Board; Angola

narrative_blocks.header: First woman on the Maine State Medical Board
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Floyd Cushman earned her medical degree from Boston University School of Medicine in 1892, practiced medicine in Massachusetts and Maine for nearly 30 years, and was appointed the first woman member of the Maine State Medical Board. Then, in 1922 at the age of 52, she sailed for Angola as a medical missionary. She had cared for her elderly mother for 20 years before making the transition to missionary work.

In 1944 she published Missionary Doctor: The Story of Twenty Years in Africa (Harper & Brothers). The New York Times called her 'a person of apparent energy and equally apparent kindliness' who accomplished a thorough success. The book remains a source on Angolan Ocimbundu culture and colonial-era medical practice.

cross_connections.display_label: an ABCFM missionary to Japan as Cushman was to Angola
her cousin and namesake, the California historian

narrative_blocks.body: William died in 1832, in his first year.

burialCemetery.name: Oakwood Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Burial place of John Paine Cushman (X01916), Maria Jones Tallmadge Cushman (HD1033), Harriet Delafield Cushman Balch (HD2816), and Rev. John Paine Cushman Jr. (HD2817).

INST024.name: Litchfield Law School
INST024.notes:
Founded 1784 by Judge Tapping Reeve (X00614) — the first law school in the United States. Tapping Reeve married Sarah "Sally" Burr Reeve (H00912), 5th-generation Hooker descendant, daughter of Esther Edwards Burr (H00378). Aaron Burr Jr. (H00913) — 5th-generation Hooker descendant — studied law here under his brother-in-law Tapping Reeve before enlisting in 1775. Among Litchfield's other notable students: John C. Calhoun (also became VP — two Vice Presidents produced by one law school). The school ran until 1833; at its peak enrolled students from across the country. Tapping Reeve House and Law School designated National Historic Landmark, 1965. In 1781, Reeve (with Theodore Sedgwick) represented Elizabeth Freeman ("Bett") in Brom & Bett v. Ashley — case that led to abolition of slavery in Massachusetts. Reeve also brought Rev. Lyman Beecher (father of Harriet Beecher Stowe) to Litchfield. The Litchfield Law School is among the most consequential institutions connected to the Hooker tree.
[hooker_connection] founded by in-law of Hooker descendant; Hooker descendant studied here

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
